nativeRange.habitat: Large river systems and floodplains with muddy or sandy substrates; slow-moving or standing water with dense vegetation and submerged woody debris; frequents shallow marginal zones

decorPreferences: fine sand or smooth gravel substrate for resting and foraging
caves, overturned clay pots, or driftwood hides — one per fish
dim or subdued lighting with floating plants to diffuse surface light
robust rooted plants or artificial plants (live planting may be uprooted)
tight-fitting lid — mandatory; bichirs are notorious escape artists

diet.notes: Obligate carnivore and piscivore. Feeds primarily by ambush and scent at night. Readily accepts meaty frozen and fresh foods: earthworms, bloodworms, prawns/shrimp, mussel, and strips of white fish or tilapia. Quality sinking carnivore pellets (e.g. Hikari Massivore Delite) accepted by many specimens once trained. Live feeder fish should be avoided due to parasite risk and reinforcement of aggression toward tankmates. Feed 2–3 times weekly — overfeeding in large carnivores causes fatty liver disease. Juveniles accept smaller chopped worm and bloodworm daily.

goodWith: large robust cichlids (Oscar, Geophagus) of similar body size
other bichir species of equal or larger size (with sufficient space and hides)
large catfish (Synodontis, large plecos) too big to be swallowed
large knife fish (with caution — similar nocturnal niche)
large datnoids or arowana in appropriately sized systems
avoidWith: any fish smaller than roughly half the bichir’s body length (will be eaten)
fin-nipping tetras or barbs (target the bichir’s dorsal finlets)
other bichirs of noticeably smaller size
invertebrates — shrimp and snails will be consumed
slow or elongated eels that may provoke jaw-lock attacks

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Polypterus delhezi — shows characteristic banded pattern on adult specimen.
Wikimedia Commons image — good lateral view showing the distinctive dark vertical banding and serrated dorsal finlets.

summary: A mid-sized Congo basin bichir (30–40 cm) with bold black vertical bands on a pale grey-green body and a row of serrated dorsal finlets. An obligate air-breather representing an ancient ray-finned lineage, it is a nocturnal ambush predator best housed with large tankmates it cannot swallow. Requires a tight-fitting lid — escape risk is high.
careNotes:
The Delhezi Bichir is one of the more tractable large bichirs for the advanced home aquarist, remaining somewhat smaller than the closely related Ornate Bichir (Polypterus ornatipinnis). It belongs to the family Polypteridae, a lineage of ancient ray-finned fishes (Actinopterygii) whose fossil record extends to the Cretaceous, and it retains a functional pair of primitive lungs (paired swim bladder lobes) that oblige it to breathe atmospheric air at the surface — without surface access, it will drown. A tight-fitting cover glass or hood with no gaps is non-negotiable: bichirs are powerful escape artists capable of forcing open loose lids and squeezing through surprisingly small openings, and found on the floor they perish quickly.

A tank of at least 400 L (footprint ≥ 150 × 60 cm) is recommended for one adult, with additional volume required for each additional bichir or large cichlid tankmate. The substrate should be fine sand or smooth gravel to protect the fish’s ventral surface and allow natural foraging behaviour. Dim lighting suits its crepuscular/nocturnal activity rhythm; floating plants or a shaded area are beneficial. Provide at least one cave or substantial hide per bichir — individuals in groups will claim territories and may jaw-lock or bite tankmates that intrude.

Water quality must be maintained diligently: bichirs tolerate a wide parameter range but are prone to bacterial infections (especially on the body surface and around the gills) when nitrates climb above ~30 ppm. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are recommended. Temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, and moderately soft-to-neutral hardness mirror the Congo basin conditions.

The jaw-locking behaviour (biting and twisting) seen toward similarly-sized competitors is a known risk in community tanks; any fish small enough to fit in the bichir’s mouth will be eaten. The Delhezi is less prone to attacking very large, robust tankmates but should be monitored for persistent fin damage to either party. Feeding after lights-out reduces competition with diurnal tankmates.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Polypterus delhezi is uncommon but documented. Successful spawnings have occurred in large planted tanks (500 L+) with a conditioning period of frequent feeding of live or high-quality frozen foods and a slight temperature rise (to ~28–29°C) simulating the onset of the wet season. Males develop broadened anal fins during the breeding season and use them to cup and fertilise eggs released by the female during spawning runs. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants; no parental care is given and adults will consume eggs and fry if not removed. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or micro-worms initially before graduating to baby brine shrimp. Raising fry to juvenile size is the primary challenge. Overall breeding difficulty is rated advanced and is not typical for the home aquarist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus delhezi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~35 cm), temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 5–20 dH, native range (upper and middle Congo basin, DRC), habitat, diet (carnivore; earthworms, prawns, frozen meaty foods), tankmate guidance, and escape-risk warning.

additional.name: FishBase — Polypterus delhezi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (reported to 40 cm TL), taxonomy (Polypteridae, Polypteriformes), native range (Congo River system), habitat (muddy-bottomed rivers and floodplains), and obligate air-breathing biology. Used 30–40 cm range spanning SeriouslyFish and FishBase maxima.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bichir Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum tank size recommendations for medium-large bichir species (100+ gal / ~380 L), substrate preferences, carnivore diet (sinking pellets, frozen meaty foods), escape-risk requirement for tight lid, and community compatibility guidelines (large tankmates only).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Bichir species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming Polypteridae as an ancient ray-finned lineage, obligate air-breathing behaviour, nocturnal activity pattern, jaw-locking aggression risk toward similarly-sized tankmates, and captive breeding accounts for Polypterus species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Polypterus species in the aquarium
additional.notes: Additional context on Delhezi Bichir banding pattern, comparison with P. ornatipinnis (Ornate Bichir, larger species), feeding advice for trained acceptance of pellets, and notes on the primitive lung physiology requiring unobstructed surface access.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Conditioning requires live/frozen meaty foods and temperature increase to ~29°C. Males use modified anal fin to fertilise scattered eggs among fine-leaved plants. Adults consume eggs and fry — remove after spawning. Fry require infusoria then baby brine shrimp. Not a typical home-aquarist breeding project.
